---
title: "Week 4: Batch loading data & list columns"
author: "Joe Nese"
institute: "University of Oregon<br>Spring 2026"
format:
  revealjs: 
    theme: default
    highlight-style: github
    incremental: false
    fig-width: 9
    fig-height: 3.5
    scrollable: true
    slide-number: true
execute: 
  echo: true
  warning: false
---

```{r}
#| include: false

library(tidyverse)
library(MetBrewer)
theme_set(theme_minimal(15))
```

# NEED TO GET DATA/SIM FROM COURSE REPO TO FOLLOW ALONG

# remove `purrr::compose()`

# explain `dplyr::nest_by()` better

# Batch Load Data & List Columns

**Week 4**

## Agenda

* Review Lab 2
* `map_dfr` 
* List columns
* Batch-loading data
* Midterm Student Survey

## Learning objectives {.smaller}

* Understand when `map_dfr` can and should be applied
* Better understand file paths, and how `{fs}` can help
* Be able to batch load data of a specific type within a mixed-type directory
* Use file names to pull data
* Introduce list columns
* Contrast: 
    + `group_by() |> nest() |> mutate() |> map()` with 
    + `nest_by() |> summarize()`
* Understand list columns and how they relate to `base::split`
* Fluently nest/unnest data frames
* Understand why `tidyr::nest()` can be a powerful framework (data frames) and when `tidyr::unnest()` can/should be used to move out of nested data frames and into a standard data frame

# Review Lab 2

# `map_dfr()`

## `map_dfr()`

If each iteration returns a data frame, you can use `map_dfr` to automatically bind all the data frames together

## Example

Create a function that simulates data (please copy the code and follow along)

```{r simulate}
#| output-location: fragment
set.seed(3000)
simulate <- function(n, mean = 0, sd = 1) {
	tibble(sample_id = seq_len(n),
	       sample = rnorm(n, mean, sd))
}
simulate(10)
```

## Two more quick examples

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
```{r}
simulate(3, 100, 10)
```
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}

```{r}
simulate(5, -10, 1.5)
```
:::

::::

## Simulation

* Assume we want to vary the sample size from 10 to 150 by increments of 5
* `mean` stays constant at 100
* `sd` is constant at 10

## Try with `purrr::map()`

`r countdown::countdown(2)`

##

```{r simulate-map}
library(tidyverse)
sims <- map(seq(10, 150, 5), simulate, mean = 100, sd = 10)
```

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}

```{r simulate-map1}
sims[1]
```
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}

```{r simulate-map2}
sims[2]
```
:::
::::

## Swap for `map_dfr`

Try it - what happens?

`r countdown::countdown(1)`

. . .

```{r simulate-map_df}
sims_df <- map_dfr(seq(10, 150, 5), simulate, 100, 10)
sims_df
```

## Notice a problem here? {.smaller}

```{r simulate-no-id}
sims_df[1:26, ]
```

## `.id`

Either a string or `NULL`

  + If a string, the output will contain a variable with that name, storing either the name (if `.x` is named) or the index (if `.x` is unnamed) of the input.
  + If `NULL`, the default, no variable will be created.

::: aside
[{purrr} documentation](https://purrr.tidyverse.org/reference/map.html)
:::

## `.id` string unnamed

```{r add-id-iteration}
sims_df2 <- map_dfr(seq(10, 150, 5), simulate, 100, 10, 
                   .id = "iteration")
sims_df2[1:15, ]
```


## `.id` named string {.smaller}

`setNames()`

- sets the names on an object and returns the object

`setNames(object = nm, nm)`

- `nm` = a character vector of names to assign to the object

. . .

```{r}
#| output-location: fragment
sample_size <- setNames(seq(10, 150, 5), 
                        english::english(seq(10, 150, 5))) 
sample_size[1:15]
```

## Try again

```{r add-id-sample_size}
sims_df3 <- map_dfr(sample_size, simulate, 100, 10, 
                   .id = "n")
sims_df3[1:15, ]
```

## `broom::tidy()`

The `{broom}` package helps us extract model output in a tidy format

```{r tidy-model}
lm(tvhours ~ age, gss_cat) |>
	broom::tidy()
```

## Fit separate models by year

Again - probably not the best *statistically*

```{r by_year}
split(gss_cat, gss_cat$year) |>
 map_dfr(
   ~lm(tvhours ~ age, .x) |>
     broom::tidy()
) 
```

## Add `.id`

In cases like the preceding, `.id` becomes invaluable

```{r by_year_id}
split(gss_cat, gss_cat$year) |>
 map_dfr(
   ~lm(tvhours ~ age, .x) |>
     broom::tidy(),
   .id = "year"
) 
```

# Batch-loading data

[**Please follow along**]{style="color:#009E73;"}

## `{fs}` functions

- `{fs}` functions always return ‘tidy’ paths
- `{fs}` functions divided into four main categories:
    + `path_`: manipulating and constructing paths
    + `file_`: files
    + `dir_`:  directories
    + `link_`: links

## `{fs}`

Could we apply `map_dfr` here?

```{r fs1}
#| output-location: fragment
# install.packages("fs")
library(fs)
dir_ls(here::here("data"))
```

. . .

```{r}
#| output-location: fragment
dir_ls(here::here("data", "pfiles_sim"))
```


## Limit files

* To be specific, we only want the `.csv`files
* That happens to be the only thing that's in there but that's not regularly the case

```{r fs2}
#| output-location: fragment
dir_ls(here::here("data", "pfiles_sim"), glob = "*.csv")
```

## Batch load

Loop through the directories and `import()` or `read_csv()`

```{r batch_load1}
#| message: false
files <- dir_ls(
  here::here("data", "pfiles_sim"), 
  glob = "*.csv"
)
batch <- map_dfr(files, read_csv)
batch
```

## Wait!

We've lost a lot of info - no way to identify which file is which

## Try to fix it!

`r countdown::countdown(2)`

## Add `.id`

. . .

Make sure you have this object

```{r add-id-batch}
#| message: false
batch2 <- map_dfr(files, read_csv, .id = "file")
batch2
```

## Rows per file

```{r count-file}
batch2 |>
	count(file) 
```

. . .

Still a bit cumbersome - What can we do?

## Remove path {.smaller}

Remove the `here::here()` path from string

```{r remove-here}
#| output-location: fragment
batch2 <- batch2 |>
	mutate(
	  file = str_replace_all(
	    file, 
	    here::here("data", "pfiles_sim", "/"), # noticed I included "/"; try it without 
	    ""
	  )
	)

batch2 |> 
  count(file)
```

## File names

```{r}
list.files(here::here("data", "pfiles_sim"))[c(1, 15, 29, 30)]
```

::: {.incremental}
- They're informative!
    + grade
    + content area
    + year (20`18`)
- Name your files thoughtfully

:::

## Pull out pieces you need

* Regular expressions are most powerful here
  	+ We haven't talked about them much
* Try [RegExplain](https://www.garrickadenbuie.com/project/regexplain/)
* Or really, just use GenAI

## Pull "*grade*" {.smaller}

- I don't expect you to know this, but you should learn how. 
- Not regex, but the idea/process
- Also, there's other ways you could extract the same info

But again, GenAI is **very** helpful for regular expression!

```{r pull-grade}
#| output-location: fragment
batch2 |>
	mutate(
	  grade = str_replace_all(
	    file, 
	    "g(\\d?\\d).+", 
	    "\\1"
    )
  ) |>
	select(file, grade)
```

## `parse_number()`

In this case `parse_number()` also works - but note that it would not work to extract the "*year*"

```{r grade-parse_number}
#| code-line-numbers: "2"
batch2 |>
	  mutate(grade = parse_number(file)) |> 
  select(file, grade)
```

## Extract "*grade*" and "*year*"

```{r parse_year}
#| code-line-numbers: "|6-8"
#| output-location: fragment
batch2 |>
	mutate(
	  grade = str_replace_all(
	    file, "g(\\d?\\d).+", "\\1"
    ),
	  year = str_replace_all( 
	    file, ".+files(\\d\\d)_sim.+", "\\1" 
    ) 
  ) |> 
	select(file, grade, year)
```

## Extract "*content*" area

```{r content}
#| code-line-numbers: "|8-10"
#| output-location: fragment
batch2 |>
	mutate(grade = str_replace_all(file, 
	                               "g(\\d?\\d).+", 
	                               "\\1"),
	       year = str_replace_all(file, 
	                              ".+files(\\d\\d)_sim.+", 
	                              "\\1"),
	       content = str_replace_all(file, 
	                                 "g\\d?\\d(.+)pfiles.+", 
	                                 "\\1")) |> 
	select(file, grade, year, content)
```

## Double checks: "*grade*"

```{r grade-double-check}
batch2 |>
	mutate(grade = str_replace_all(file, 
	                               "g(\\d?\\d).+", 
	                               "\\1"),
	       year = str_replace_all(file, 
	                              ".+files(\\d\\d)_sim.+", 
	                              "\\1"),
	       content = str_replace_all(file, 
	                                 "/g\\d?\\d(.+)pfiles.+", 
	                                 "\\1")) |>
	select(file, grade, year, content) |>
	count(grade)
```

## Double checks: "*year*"

```{r year-double-check}
batch2 |>
	mutate(grade = str_replace_all(file, 
	                               "g(\\d?\\d).+", 
	                               "\\1"),
	       year = str_replace_all(file, 
	                              ".+files(\\d\\d)_sim.+", 
	                              "\\1"),
	       content = str_replace_all(file, 
	                                 "/g\\d?\\d(.+)pfiles.+", 
	                                 "\\1")) |>
	select(file, grade, year, content) |>
	count(year)
```

## Double checks: "*content*"

```{r content-double-check}
batch2 |>
	mutate(grade = str_replace_all(file, 
	                               "g(\\d?\\d).+", 
	                               "\\1"),
	       year = str_replace_all(file, 
	                              ".+files(\\d\\d)_sim.+", 
	                              "\\1"),
	       content = str_replace_all(file, 
	                                 "g\\d?\\d(.+)pfiles.+", 
	                                 "\\1")) |>
	select(file, grade, year, content) |>
	count(content)
```

## Finalize

Copy & run this code

```{r finalize-batch2}
d <- batch2 |>
	mutate(
	  grade = str_replace_all(
	    file, "g(\\d?\\d).+", "\\1"
    ),
	  grade = as.integer(grade),
	  year = str_replace_all(
	    file, ".+files(\\d\\d)_sim.+", "\\1"
    ),
	  year = as.integer(grade),
	  content = str_replace_all(
	    file, "g\\d?\\d(.+)pfiles.+", "\\1"
    )
  ) |>
	select(
	  ssid, grade, year, content, testeventid, 
	  asmtprmrydsbltycd, asmtscndrydsbltycd, Entry:WMLE
  )
```

## Final product

* In this case, we basically have a tidy data frame already! 
* We've reduced our problem from 31 files to a single file

```{r print-final}
d
```

## Quick look at distributions {.smaller}

```{r fig}
#| output-location: fragment
library(ggridges)
ggplot(d, aes(x = Theta, y = factor(grade))) +
	geom_density_ridges(fill = "dodgerblue", alpha = 0.8, bandwidth = 0.3) +
	facet_wrap(~content, nrow = 1)
```

## Summary stats

```{r summary-stats}
d |>
	group_by(grade, content, asmtprmrydsbltycd) |>
	summarize(mean = mean(Theta)) |>
	pivot_wider(names_from = content, 
	            values_from = mean)
```

## Backing up a bit

What if we wanted only math files?

```{r math}
#| output-location: fragment
dir_ls(here::here("data", "pfiles_sim"), regexp = "Math")
```

## Only Grade 5 files?

You try

`r countdown::countdown(3)`

. . .

```{r g5}
#| code-line-numbers: "3"
g5_paths <- dir_ls(
  here::here("data", "pfiles_sim"), 
  regexp = "g5" 
)
```

## The rest is the same

```{r }
#| message: false
#| output-location: fragment
g5 <- map_dfr(g5_paths, read_csv, .id = "file") |>
  mutate(
	  file = str_replace_all(
	    file, 
	    here::here("data", "pfiles_sim", "/"), 
	    ""
	  )
	)
g5
```


## base equivalents

```{r list.files1}
#| output-location: fragment
list.files(here::here("data", "pfiles_sim"))
```

## Full path

`full.names = TRUE`

```{r list.files2}
#| output-location: fragment
#| code-line-numbers: "|2"
list.files(here::here("data", "pfiles_sim"),
           full.names = TRUE)
```

## Only .csv

`pattern = "__"`

```{r list.files-csvs}
#| output-location: fragment
#| code-line-numbers: "|3"
list.files(here::here("data", "pfiles_sim"), 
           full.names = TRUE,
           pattern = ".csv")

```

## So why not use base? {.smaller}

* We could, but `{fs}` plays a little nicer with `{purrr}`

. . .

```{r list.files-id-fail}
#| error: true
files <- list.files(
  here::here("data", "pfiles_sim"), 
  pattern = "*.csv"
)
batch3 <- map_dfr(files, read_csv, .id = "file")
```

. . .

Need to return full names

```{r files-not-full-names}
files
```

## Try again

```{r list.files-id-success}
#| message: false
files <- list.files(here::here("data", "pfiles_sim"), 
                    pattern = "*.csv", 
                    full.names = TRUE)
batch3 <- map_dfr(files, read_csv, .id = "file")
batch3
```

## indexes

The prior example gave us indexes, rather than the file path. Why?

. . .

`files` does not have names

```{r vector-names}
names(files)
```

. . .

We **need** the file path! An index isn't nearly as useful

## Base method that works

```{r }
#| message: false
#| output-location: fragment
#| code-line-numbers: "|1-3|4|6-7"
files <- list.files(here::here("data", "pfiles_sim"), 
                    pattern = "*.csv", 
                    full.names = TRUE) 
files <- setNames(files, files)

batch4 <- map_dfr(files, read_csv, .id = "file")
batch4
```

## My recommendation

* If you're working interactively, no reason **not** to use `{fs}`
* If you are building **functions** that take paths, might be worth considering skipping the package dependency
* I am **not** saying don't use `{fs}`, but just **consider** whether it is really needed or not

# List columns

## Comparing models

Let's say we wanted to fit/compare a set of models for each content area

1. `lm(Theta ~ asmtprmrydsbltycd)`

1. `lm(Theta ~ asmtprmrydsbltycd + asmtscndrydsbltycd)`

1. `lm(Theta ~ asmtprmrydsbltycd * asmtscndrydsbltycd)`

## Data pre-processing

* The disability variables are stored as numbers, we need them as factors
* We'll make the names easier in the process

```{r disab-recode}
d <- d |>
	mutate(primary = as.factor(asmtprmrydsbltycd),
	       secondary = as.factor(asmtscndrydsbltycd))
```

## Split the data

The base method we've been using...

```{r split}
splt_content <- split(d, d$content)
str(splt_content)
```

## We could use this method

Then conduct tests to see which model fit better, etc.

```{r fit-models-split}
m1 <- map(
  splt_content, 
  ~lm(Theta ~ asmtprmrydsbltycd, data = .x)
)

m2 <- map(
  splt_content, 
  ~lm(Theta ~ asmtprmrydsbltycd + asmtscndrydsbltycd, 
      data = .x)
)

m3 <- map(
  splt_content, 
  ~lm(Theta ~ asmtprmrydsbltycd * asmtscndrydsbltycd, 
      data = .x)
)
```



## Alternative

Create a data frame with a list column

```{r list-column}
#| output-location: fragment
(by_content <- d |>
  group_by(content) |> 
  nest())
```

## What's going on here?

```{r list-column-listed}
str(by_content$data)
```

## Explore a bit

```{r map-data1}
#| output-location: fragment
map_dbl(by_content$data, nrow)
```


```{r}
#| output-location: fragment
map_dbl(by_content$data, ncol)
```


```{r}
#| output-location: fragment
map_dbl(by_content$data, ~mean(.x$Theta))
```

## What's going on here?

My go-to method

```{r}
by_content$data[[1]]
```

## It's a data frame!

We can add these summaries if we want

```{r n-by-content}
#| output-location: fragment
by_content |>
	mutate(n = map_dbl(data, nrow))
```

## `map_*`

* Note on the previous example we used `map_dbl` and we got a vector in return
* What would happen if we just used `map`?

. . .

```{r n-by-content-list}
by_content |>
	mutate(n = map(data, nrow))
```

## Let's fit a model!

```{r list-column-model}
#| code-line-numbers: "|2"
by_content |>
	mutate(m1 = map(data, ~lm(Theta ~ primary, data = .x)))
```

## Extract the coefficients

```{r }
#| code-line-numbers: "|4"
by_content |>
  mutate(
    m1 = map(data, ~lm(Theta ~ primary, data = .x)),
    coefs = map(m1, coef)
)
```


## 

![](https://media0.giphy.com/media/v1.Y2lkPTc5MGI3NjExaTVhbW1mbm03NWQ5Zmx4M2JlNWVqMHIwYmtjdnRkOGgyb3JqOTYxcyZlcD12MV9pbnRlcm5hbF9naWZfYnlfaWQmY3Q9Zw/xT9IgEldeKnHa9oOCQ/giphy.gif)


## Challenge {.smaller}

* Output the mean score for students who were coded as not having a disability (code 0),
along with students coded as having TBI.
* In other words, continue the previous slide, but output a data frame with three columns:
    (1) `content`
    (2) `intercept`, and 
    (3) `tbi` coefficient (which is primary code 74, "*primary74*")

`r countdown::countdown(4)`

## Solution

. . .

```{r}
by_content |>
  mutate(
    m1 = map(data, ~lm(Theta ~ primary, data = .x)),
    coefs = map(m1, coef),
    no_disab = map_dbl(coefs, 1),
    tbi = map_dbl(coefs, "primary74")
  ) |>
  select(content, no_disab, tbi)
```

## Solution

To be pedantic (accurate) about it, to get the effect of the `tbi` variable, we'd need to add the `intercept` to the `tbi` coefficient (don't worry about this if you don't do regression)

```{r}
by_content |>
  mutate(
    m1 = map(data, ~lm(Theta ~ primary, data = .x)),
    coefs = map(m1, coef),
    no_disab = map_dbl(coefs, 1),
    tbi = no_disab + map_dbl(coefs, "primary74")
  ) |>
  select(content, no_disab, tbi)
```

## Compare models

Back to our original task - fit all three models

You try first

1. `lm(Theta ~ primary)`

2. `lm(Theta ~ primary + secondary)`

3. `lm(Theta ~ primary + secondary + primary:secondary)`

`r countdown::countdown(4)`

## Model fits

. . .

```{r three-mods}
#| output-location: fragment
mods <- by_content |>
  mutate(
    m1 = map(data, ~lm(Theta ~ primary, data = .x)),
    m2 = map(data, ~lm(Theta ~ primary + secondary, data = .x)),
    m3 = map(data, ~lm(Theta ~ primary * secondary, data = .x))
)
mods
```

## Brief walk into parallel iterations

`stats::anova()` function can compare the fit of two models

- computes analysis of variance (ANOVA) tables for one or more fitted model objects
- test whether the model terms are significant
- provide a summary for a single model's significance
- or compare multiple models against each other

## But first...

How would we extract model 1 and model 2 for ELA data?

. . .

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}

```{r ela-mods1}
mods$m1[[1]]
```
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}

```{r ela-mods2}
mods$m2[[1]]
```
:::

::::

## So which fits better? {.smaller}

```{r anova}
#| output-location: fragment
compare <- anova(mods$m1[[1]], mods$m2[[1]])
compare
```

. . .

`Pr(>F)` $p$ < 0.001 so we reject the null hypothesis (means are equal) and conclude that group means differ

## `map2()` {.smaller}

::: {.incremental}
* Works the same as `map` but iterates over two vectors concurrently
    + You heard that right, iterates over two vectors concurrently!
* Let's compare model 1 and 2

:::

. . .

```{r mod_compare1}
mods |>
  mutate(comp12 = map2(m1, m2, anova))
```

. . .

Ok...now what?

## Back to our original `anova()` object

Can we pull out  useful things?

```{r str-comp}
str(compare)
```

. . .

Try pulling out the $p$-value

## Extract $p$ value

*Note* - I'd recommend looking at more than just a $p$-value, but I do think this is useful for a quick glance

```{r extract-p1}
compare$`Pr(>F)`
compare[["Pr(>F)"]]
```

. . .

```{r extract-p2}
compare$`Pr(>F)`[2]
compare[["Pr(>F)"]][2]
```

## Back to our list columns {.smaller}

Extract all $p$-values

This is probably the most compact syntax, but that doesn't mean it's the most clear

```{r pull-pvals12}
mods |>
  mutate(comp12 = map2(m1, m2, anova),
         p12 = map_dbl(comp12, list("Pr(>F)", 2)))
```

. . .

I'd probably do

```{r}
mods |>
  mutate(comp12 = map2(m1, m2, anova),
         p12 = map_dbl(comp12, ~.x[["Pr(>F)"]][2]))
```

## Another approach

Write a function that pulls the $p$-value from model comparison objects

. . .

```{r fun-compare}
fx_extractp <- function(anova_ob) {
  anova_ob[["Pr(>F)"]][2]
}
```

. . .

Loop this function through the anova objects

```{r pull-pvals12-fun}
#| output-location: fragment
#| code-line-numbers: "|3"
mods |>
  mutate(comp12 = map2(m1, m2, anova),
         p12 = map_dbl(comp12, fx_extractp))
```

## Brief sidetrack

We can also create the function using `purrr::compose()`

. . .

![](https://media4.giphy.com/media/v1.Y2lkPTc5MGI3NjExb3NycWJ2Mmd0aDBtdmYwZnh3Z2JnM3EyZWh1OGg1OXJmamVkbTdoNiZlcD12MV9pbnRlcm5hbF9naWZfYnlfaWQmY3Q9Zw/f6cyISseQU9IoW9wW5/giphy.gif)

## `compose()` {.smaller}

[`compose()`](https://purrr.tidyverse.org/reference/compose.html)

Create a new function that is the composition of multiple functions

- i.e., `compose(f, g)` is equivalent to `f(g(...))`

. . .

```{r}
not_null <- compose(`!`, is.null)
```

. . .

```{r}
#| output-location: fragment
not_null(4)
```

. . .

```{r}
#| output-location: fragment
not_null(NULL)
```

. . .

```{r}
#| output-location: fragment
add1 <- function(x) x + 1
compose(add1, add1)(8)
```

## `compose()` example

Create a centering function (which subtracts the mean from each observation)

Use `~` and `.x`, just like with the `map()` functions

```{r }
fx_center <- compose(~.x - mean(.x, na.rm = TRUE))
```

## Test it out

```{r}
#| output-location: fragment
library(palmerpenguins)

penguins$bill_length_mm |> 
  head()
```

. . .

```{r}
#| output-location: fragment
#| code-line-numbers: "|2"
penguins$bill_length_mm |>
  fx_center() |> 
  head()
```

. . .

```{r}
#| output-location: fragment
penguins$bill_length_mm |> 
  fx_center() |>
  mean(na.rm = TRUE) |>
  round()
```

## Compose a $p$-value extractor

```{r }
fx_p <- compose(~.x[["Pr(>F)"]][2])
```

. . .

Apply it

```{r }
#| output-location: fragment
mods |>
  mutate(
    comp12 = map2(m1, m2, anova),
    p12 = map_dbl(comp12, fx_p)
  )
```

## Functions

* This was a quick intro
* Don't worry if it doesn't really make sense yet
* We'll talk about them a lot more in the coming weeks

# Conducting operations by row

A `{purrr}` alternative

## Operations by row {.smaller}

The `dplyr::rowwise()` function fundamentally changes the way a `tibble()` behaves

```{r }
(df <- tibble(name = c("Me", "You"), x = 1:2, y = 3:4, z = 5:6))
```

. . .

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="50%" .fragment}
```{r }
df |> 
  mutate(m = mean(c(x, y, z)))
```

:::

::: {.column width="50%" .fragment}

```{r }
#| code-line-numbers: "|2"
df |> 
  rowwise() |> 
  mutate(m = mean(c(x, y, z)))
```
:::

::::

## Add a group & summarize

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="50%"}

```{r }
df |> 
  rowwise() |> 
  summarize(m = mean(c(x, y, z)))
```

:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
```{r }
df |> 
  rowwise(name) |> 
  summarize(m = mean(c(x, y, z)))
```
:::

::::

## List columns {.smaller}

If you apply `rowwise()` operation with a list column, you don't have to loop

```{r }
(df <- tibble(var = list(1, 2:3, 4:6)))
```

. . .

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%" .fragment}

`map_`

```{r }
df |> 
  mutate(
    lngth = map_int(var, length)
  )
```
:::

::: {.column width="50%".fragment}

`rowwise()`

```{r }
df |> 
  rowwise() |> 
  mutate(lnght = length(var))
```
:::

::::

# List columns again

## Creating list columns {.smaller}

You can use the `dplyr::nest_by()` function to create a list column for each group, *and* convert it to a rowwise data frame

. . .

```{r }
#| output-location: fragment
d |> 
  nest_by(content)
```

. . .

Change the column name

```{r }
#| output-location: fragment
d |> 
  nest_by(content, .key = "items")
```

## Challenge {.smaller}

Can you fit a model of the form `lm(Theta ~ primary)` to each content area in the `d` object we created using `nest_by()`, **NOT** using `{purrr}`?

`r countdown::countdown(2)`

. . .

Wrap it in `list()` (should suggest this in the error reporting if you didn't do it)

```{r }
d |> 
  nest_by(content) |> 
  mutate(m1 = list(lm(Theta ~ primary, data = data)))
```

## Another Challenge {.smaller}

Can you extend it further and extract the coefficients with `coef`? 

What about creating a new column that has the intercept values?

`r countdown::countdown(2)`

. . .

```{r }
d |> 
  nest_by(content) |> 
  mutate(m1 = list(lm(Theta ~ primary, data = data)),
         coefs = list(coef(m1)),
         intercept = coef(m1)[1])
```

## Fit all models

This gets us the same results we got before:

```{r }
mods2 <- d |>
  nest_by(content) |> 
  mutate(
    m1 = list(lm(Theta ~ primary, data = data)),
    m2 = list(lm(Theta ~ primary + secondary, data = data)),
    m3 = list(lm(Theta ~ primary * secondary, data = data))
)
mods2
```

## Look at all $R^2$

It's a normal tidy data frame!

```{r gather-models}
mods |>
  pivot_longer(
    m1:m3,
    names_to = "model",
    values_to = "output"
)
```

## Extract all $R^2$ {.smaller}

*Note*: might rather want to write a function here

```{r extract-r2}
r2 <- mods |>
  pivot_longer(
    m1:m3,
    names_to = "model",
    values_to = "output"
  ) |> 
  mutate(r2 = map_dbl(output, ~summary(.x)$r.squared))
r2
```

## Plot {.smaller}

```{r model-plot}
ggplot(r2, aes(model, r2)) +
	geom_col(aes(fill = model)) +
	facet_wrap(~content) +
	guides(fill = "none") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = met.brewer("Egypt", 3))
```

## Unnesting

* Sometimes you just want to `unnest()`
* Imagine we want to plot the coefficients by model... how?
* `broom::tidy()` & `tidyr::unnest()`

## Tidy

```{r}
#| code-line-numbers: "|7"
#| output-location: fragment
mods |>
	pivot_longer(
	  m1:m3,
	  names_to = "model", 
	  values_to = "output"
  ) |>
	mutate(tidied = map(output, broom::tidy))
```

## Equivalently

```{r}
#| code-line-numbers: "|7|8"
mods |>
	pivot_longer(
	  m1:m3,
	  names_to = "model", 
	  values_to = "output"
  ) |>
  rowwise() |> 
  mutate(tidied = list(broom::tidy(output)))
```

## Select and unnest

```{r tidied}
#| code-line-numbers: "|8|9"
#| output-location: fragment
tidied <- mods |>
	pivot_longer(
	  m1:m3,
	  names_to = "model", 
	  values_to = "output"
  ) |> 
	mutate(tidy_broom = map(output, broom::tidy)) |>
	select(content, model, tidy_broom) |>
	unnest(tidy_broom)
tidied
```

## Plot

Lets look how the primary coefficients change

```{r coef-plot-echo}
#| output-location: slide
to_plot <- names(coef(mods$m1[[1]]))

tidied |>
  filter(term %in% to_plot) |>
  ggplot(aes(estimate, term, color = model)) +
  geom_point() +
  scale_color_manual(values = met.brewer("Egypt", 3)) +
  facet_wrap(~content, nrow = 1)
```

## Last bit

* We've kind of been running the wrong models this whole time
* We forgot about "*grade*"!
* No problem, just change the grouping factor

## By grade

```{r by_grade}
#| output-location: fragment
by_grade_content <- d |>
  group_by(content, grade) |>
  nest()
by_grade_content
```

## Fit models

```{r by_grade_mods}
#| output-location: fragment
mods_grade <- by_grade_content |>
  mutate(
    m1 = map(data, ~lm(Theta ~ primary, data = .x)),
    m2 = map(data, ~lm(Theta ~ primary + secondary, 
                       data = .x)),
    m3 = map(data, ~lm(Theta ~ primary * secondary, 
                       data = .x))
)
mods_grade
```

## Look at $R^2$

```{r by_grade_r2}
#| output-location: fragment
mods_grade |>
	pivot_longer(
    m1:m3, 
    names_to = "model", 
    values_to = "output"
  ) |> 
	mutate(r2 = map_dbl(output, ~summary(.x)$r.squared))
```

## Plot {.smaller}

```{r by_grade_r2_plot-echo}
#| output-location: fragment
mods_grade |>
  pivot_longer(
    m1:m3, 
    names_to = "model", 
    values_to = "output"
  ) |> 
  mutate(r2 = map_dbl(output, ~summary(.x)$r.squared)) |>
  ggplot(aes(model, r2)) +
  geom_col(aes(fill = model)) +
  facet_grid(grade ~ content) +
  guides(fill = "none") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = met.brewer("Egypt", 3))
```

## Summary {.smaller}

* List columns are really powerful and really flexible
* Also help you stay organized
* You can approach the problem either with `{purrr}` or `dplyr::rowwise()` 
    + **Important**: If you use `rowwise()`, remember to `ungroup()` when you want it to go back to being a normal data frame
    + `rowwise()` might be a bit easier but is a little less general (only works with data frames)
    + I always use `{purrr}`

# Next time

## Before next class

- No readings!
- Midterm Student Survey now

# Midterm Student Survey

## Midterm Student Survey

- Log onto Canvas
- Click "UO Course Surveys" on the left bar
- Complete survey
